quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 10 Quiz 7
quiz.subtitle: Magnetic Materials and Properties of Magnets
quiz.subject: Physics

questions.question: One of the following is a ferromagnetic material:
questions.explanation: Iron is a ferromagnetic material — it is strongly attracted to magnets and can be permanently magnetized. Copper is diamagnetic, while aluminium and oxygen are paramagnetic.

questions.question: ____________ is a fragile type of magnetism generated by a change in the electrons' orbital motion due to an enforced magnetic field.
questions.explanation: Diamagnetism is a weak, fragile type of magnetism caused by the alteration of electrons' orbital motion when an external magnetic field is applied. It results in weak repulsion from the field. All materials exhibit diamagnetism, but it is often masked by stronger effects.

questions.question: A magnet is any material that has the capability to attract __________ and produce magnetic fields around itself.
questions.explanation: A magnet is a material that attracts iron (and other ferromagnetic materials like nickel and cobalt) and produces a magnetic field in the space around itself. This is the fundamental definition of a magnet.

questions.question: Temporary magnets consist of ________ metals.
questions.explanation: Temporary magnets are made from magnetically soft metals (like soft iron). These materials are easily magnetized but also easily lose their magnetism when the external magnetic field is removed.

questions.question: Temporary magnets:
options: Gain and lose magnetism easily in response to degree of presence in magnetic field
Gain easily but hardly lose magnetism in response to presence in magnetic field
Gain hardly, but lose magnetism easily in response to presence in magnetic field
Gain and lose magnetism very hardly in response to presence in magnetic field
questions.explanation: Temporary magnets (made of magnetically soft materials) gain magnetism easily when placed in a magnetic field and lose it easily when the field is removed. This makes them ideal for electromagnets.

questions.question: The process of becoming a permanent magnet is known as ____________
questions.explanation: Magnetic saturation is the state reached when an increase in the applied external magnetic field cannot increase the magnetization of the material further — it has become fully/permanently magnetized. (Note: Some contexts may also refer to 'magnetic hardening' as the process of making permanent magnets.)

questions.question: Magnetism is one of __________ aspects of electromagnetism.
questions.explanation: Magnetism is one of two aspects of electromagnetism. The two aspects are electricity and magnetism, which are fundamentally interrelated — together they form the electromagnetic force.

questions.question: The process of losing magnetic property is known as ______________
questions.explanation: Demagnetization is the process by which a magnet loses its magnetic properties. This can occur through heating, hammering, or placing the magnet in a strong alternating magnetic field.

questions.question: Aluminium and oxygen are classified as ___________ materials.
questions.explanation: Aluminium and oxygen are paramagnetic materials. They are weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field and do not retain magnetism when the field is removed.

questions.question: _____________ substances are weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field.
questions.explanation: Paramagnetic substances (e.g., aluminium, oxygen, platinum) are weakly attracted to an applied magnetic field. This is because they have unpaired electrons that align with the external field.

questions.question: _____________ substances are weakly repelled by an applied magnetic field.
questions.explanation: Diamagnetic substances (e.g., copper, water, bismuth) are weakly repelled by an applied magnetic field. This occurs due to induced changes in the orbital motion of electrons.

questions.question: The strength of a magnetic field always decreases with ___________
questions.explanation: The strength of a magnetic field decreases with increasing distance from the source. This follows the inverse square law for point sources (or inverse cube for dipoles) — the farther away you are, the weaker the field.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered magnetic materials, types of magnetism, and the properties of magnets!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the differences between diamagnetic, paramagnetic, and ferromagnetic materials.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on temporary vs permanent magnets and the classification of magnetic materials.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: types of magnetic materials (ferromagnetic, paramagnetic, diamagnetic), temporary vs permanent magnets, and how magnetic field strength changes with distance.
